diagnosis: Peptic ulcer disease with upper GI bleed
accepted_answers: PUD bleed; bleeding peptic ulcer; duodenal ulcer bleed; gastric ulcer bleed
clue_1: A 63-year-old comes in for fatigue, mild dizziness when standing, and darker stools that he initially blamed on iron tablets.
clue_2: He has had intermittent upper abdominal discomfort for months, often quieting down after a snack or antacid.
clue_3: He takes ibuprofen most days for knee pain and recently added low-dose aspirin after a vascular event.
clue_4: Rectal exam shows black, sticky stool; hemoglobin is below his baseline and the BUN has risen more than the creatinine.
clue_5: He has no ascites, splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, or heavy retching before the bleeding started.
clue_6: Upper endoscopy localizes an actively oozing crater in the stomach or duodenal bulb.
explanation: Bleeding peptic ulcer disease is the most common upper GI bleed source and is supported by H. pylori or NSAID risk, epigastric pain, melena, and an elevated BUN:Cr ratio from digested blood protein.
high_yield: H. pylori/NSAIDs; epigastric pain; melena or coffee-ground emesis; BUN:Cr >20; endoscopy diagnostic and therapeutic.

accepted_answers: variceal bleed; bleeding esophageal varices; portal hypertensive varices
clue_1: A 56-year-old is brought in after suddenly filling a basin with blood, with surprisingly little abdominal pain beforehand.
clue_2: Family says his abdomen has been gradually enlarging and he bruises easily; he has had prior episodes of confusion.
clue_3: Exam shows scleral icterus, shifting dullness, palmar erythema, and dilated abdominal wall veins.
clue_4: Labs show thrombocytopenia, elevated INR, low albumin, and a mildly increased bilirubin.
clue_5: The bleeding is brisk and painless enough to cause orthostasis within minutes.
clue_6: Urgent endoscopy would reveal large distal esophageal venous columns under high portal pressure.
explanation: Portal hypertension dilates the esophageal venous plexus, and rupture causes painless, often massive hematemesis in a patient with cirrhosis and coagulopathy.
high_yield: Cirrhosis/portal HTN; painless large-volume hematemesis; thrombocytopenia; elevated INR; highest-mortality UGIB pattern.

accepted_answers: Mallory Weiss tear; GE junction mucosal tear; post-retching hematemesis
clue_1: A 34-year-old comes in after a night of severe nausea, initially vomiting only food and gastric contents.
clue_2: Only after repeated forceful retching did bright red streaks appear in the emesis.
clue_3: He has mild epigastric soreness but no rigid abdomen, melena history, or liver disease stigmata.
clue_4: Hemoglobin is near baseline and vital signs improve with fluids.
clue_5: Platelets, INR, and liver chemistries are unremarkable.
clue_6: Endoscopy would show a linear mucosal tear near the gastroesophageal junction.
explanation: A Mallory-Weiss tear is a longitudinal mucosal tear at the gastroesophageal junction caused by sudden increases in intraabdominal pressure from retching.
high_yield: Vomiting first, then hematemesis; GE junction linear tear; alcoholism, bulimia, hyperemesis; usually self-limited.

accepted_answers: cholecystitis; acute calculous cholecystitis; cystic duct obstruction
clue_1: A 42-year-old reports persistent right upper abdominal discomfort that began after a heavy meal and did not fade like prior episodes.
clue_2: She has nausea and pain with bumps in the car, but the first description sounds like nonspecific indigestion.
clue_3: Temperature is 38.2 C and she stops inspiration when the examiner presses beneath the right costal margin.
clue_4: White count is elevated while bilirubin and transaminases are only mildly abnormal.
clue_5: Ultrasound shows stones plus gallbladder wall thickening and pericholecystic fluid rather than only a mobile stone.
clue_6: The obstructed duct is the cystic duct, causing inflamed gallbladder wall and constant pain.
explanation: Acute cholecystitis is inflammation from cystic duct obstruction, producing constant RUQ pain, fever, Murphy sign, and gallbladder inflammatory changes on ultrasound.
high_yield: Constant RUQ pain; right shoulder/scapula radiation; fever; Murphy sign; gallstones + wall thickening/pericholecystic fluid.

accepted_answers: cholangitis; acute ascending cholangitis; infected choledocholithiasis
clue_1: A 71-year-old with known gallstones arrives weak and confused after a day of vague upper abdominal pain.
clue_2: He is febrile with rigors and his family noticed his eyes looked yellow this morning.
clue_3: Labs show direct hyperbilirubinemia with ALP and GGT much higher than AST and ALT.
clue_4: Ultrasound shows a dilated common bile duct rather than isolated gallbladder inflammation.
clue_5: Blood pressure begins to fall, adding septic physiology to the obstructive picture.
clue_6: The needed intervention is biliary decompression, usually by ERCP, for infected obstruction.
explanation: Ascending cholangitis occurs when an obstructed biliary system becomes infected, causing RUQ pain, fever, jaundice, and sometimes shock or altered mental status.
high_yield: Charcot triad = fever + RUQ pain + jaundice; Reynold pentad adds hypotension and AMS; ALP/GGT/direct bilirubin high; dilated CBD; ERCP treats.

accepted_answers: acute hepatocellular hepatitis; acute liver injury; acute viral hepatitis pattern
clue_1: A 24-year-old presents with several days of malaise, poor appetite, nausea, and a heavy feeling under the right ribs.
clue_2: He later notices tea-colored urine and yellowing of the eyes after what felt like a viral syndrome.
clue_3: Exam shows tender hepatomegaly without peritoneal signs or focal gallbladder tenderness.
clue_4: AST and ALT are both in the thousands, far out of proportion to ALP.
clue_5: Ultrasound does not show biliary dilation or gallstones causing obstruction.
clue_6: Serologies or toxin testing would identify hepatocellular injury rather than a ductal process.
explanation: Acute hepatitis causes hepatocellular injury with prodrome, jaundice, RUQ discomfort, and very high transaminases, often far exceeding the cholestatic enzymes.
high_yield: Viral/drug/toxic exposure; malaise and anorexia; AST/ALT often >1000; jaundice/dark urine; rule out obstruction.

accepted_answers: perforated ulcer; perforated duodenal ulcer; perforated gastric ulcer
clue_1: A 59-year-old with long-standing dyspepsia develops sudden severe abdominal pain while eating, then lies still because movement worsens it.
clue_2: He has a history of NSAID use and prior epigastric symptoms, but the current pain is abrupt and maximal.
clue_3: Exam shows guarding, rebound, and absent bowel sounds rather than isolated RUQ tenderness.
clue_4: White count is elevated and he appears toxic despite only a short symptom duration.
clue_5: Upright chest film or CT shows free intraperitoneal air.
clue_6: A full-thickness gastric or duodenal ulcer has leaked contents into the peritoneal cavity.
explanation: Full-thickness ulcer perforation spills gastric or duodenal contents into the peritoneum, causing sudden severe pain, rigid abdomen, peritonitis, and free intraperitoneal air.
high_yield: Sudden maximal pain; rigid abdomen; peritonitis; H. pylori/NSAIDs; free air under diaphragm = perforation.

accepted_answers: GERD; reflux esophagitis; erosive esophagitis
clue_1: A 38-year-old comes to family medicine for chronic throat clearing, cough at night, and intermittent chest discomfort after meals.
clue_2: Cardiac workup has been unrevealing and symptoms worsen when he lies down after late dinners.
clue_3: He reports a bitter taste in the mouth and occasional regurgitation, but no true progressive dysphagia.
clue_4: Symptoms improve with antacids and a short proton pump inhibitor trial.
clue_5: He is obese and drinks coffee daily; exam is largely normal aside from dental enamel wear.
clue_6: The mechanism is reflux from an incompetent lower esophageal sphincter causing esophageal irritation.
explanation: GERD results from lower esophageal sphincter dysfunction with acid reflux, producing postprandial and positional burning pain with regurgitation.
high_yield: Burning substernal/epigastric pain; worse after meals or lying flat; sour regurgitation; antacid/PPI response; alarm symptoms need endoscopy.

accepted_answers: pancreatitis; gallstone pancreatitis; alcoholic pancreatitis
clue_1: A 44-year-old presents with severe upper abdominal pain and vomiting after a weekend of heavy alcohol intake.
clue_2: He cannot get comfortable lying flat and prefers sitting forward, but the initial exam is just epigastric tenderness.
clue_3: The pain travels straight through to the back and vomiting does not relieve it.
clue_4: Lipase returns more than three times the upper limit of normal.
clue_5: BUN rises during observation, raising concern for more severe disease.
clue_6: Autodigestion from prematurely activated pancreatic enzymes is driving the presentation.
explanation: Acute pancreatitis is pancreatic enzyme activation causing severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, relief leaning forward, and lipase elevation above three times normal.
high_yield: Epigastric pain to back; better leaning forward; lipase >3x ULN; gallstones/alcohol; ALT >150 suggests gallstone cause.

accepted_answers: acute gastritis; chronic gastritis; H pylori gastritis; autoimmune gastritis
clue_1: A 51-year-old has weeks of vague epigastric burning, early satiety, nausea, and bloating without a single dramatic bleeding episode.
clue_2: Symptoms fluctuate with alcohol and intermittent NSAID use but are not clearly relieved or worsened by meals in a classic ulcer pattern.
clue_3: Exam shows mild epigastric tenderness and no peritoneal signs.
clue_4: CBC may show mild anemia; H. pylori testing is considered.
clue_5: Endoscopy shows diffusely inflamed gastric mucosa without a discrete crater penetrating through the muscularis mucosa.
clue_6: If autoimmune, B12 deficiency, achlorhydria, high gastrin, and intrinsic factor antibodies may appear.
explanation: Gastritis is gastric mucosal inflammation without a discrete ulcer; H. pylori, NSAIDs, alcohol, and autoimmune parietal cell destruction are common causes.
high_yield: Epigastric discomfort/nausea; no discrete ulcer on endoscopy; H. pylori/NSAIDs; autoimmune gastritis causes B12 deficiency, achlorhydria, high gastrin.

accepted_answers: appendicitis; inflamed appendix
clue_1: A 19-year-old initially complains of vague periumbilical discomfort and loss of appetite, thinking it is a stomach bug.
clue_2: Several hours later the pain localizes to the right lower abdomen and walking becomes uncomfortable.
clue_3: Nausea follows the pain rather than preceding it; low-grade fever develops.
clue_4: Exam shows focal RLQ tenderness with pain on cough and possible psoas or obturator irritation.
clue_5: CBC shows leukocytosis with left shift, and pregnancy/urinary causes are excluded when relevant.
clue_6: CT or ultrasound would show an inflamed obstructed appendix before perforation.
explanation: Appendiceal lumen obstruction causes distension and inflammation, classically producing periumbilical pain that migrates to the RLQ with anorexia and leukocytosis.
high_yield: Periumbilical to RLQ migration; anorexia; pain before vomiting; McBurney/Rovsing/psoas/obturator signs; CT gold standard in nonpregnant adults.

accepted_answers: Crohn; Crohn's disease; regional enteritis; transmural IBD
clue_1: A 23-year-old has months of intermittent crampy abdominal pain, fatigue, and loose stools that are usually not frankly bloody.
clue_2: He has lost weight and notices pain in the right lower abdomen after meals.
clue_3: Exam reveals aphthous ulcers and a tender fullness in the RLQ; perianal skin tags are present.
clue_4: Labs show elevated CRP, low albumin, and anemia that may include B12 deficiency.
clue_5: Enterography shows separated inflamed segments with thickened bowel wall and a possible fistulous tract.
clue_6: Colonoscopy would show skip lesions and transmural granulomatous inflammation, often involving terminal ileum.
explanation: Crohn disease is transmural, skip-lesion inflammatory bowel disease that commonly involves the terminal ileum and causes fistulas, strictures, perianal disease, and nonbloody diarrhea.
high_yield: Transmural; skip lesions; terminal ileum/RLQ; fistulas/strictures/perianal disease; ASCA; smoking worsens; B12 deficiency possible.

accepted_answers: hemolysis; hemolytic jaundice; prehepatic jaundice
clue_1: A 28-year-old presents with fatigue, mild jaundice, and dark urine after a recent infection.
clue_2: He has pallor and splenomegaly but no RUQ pain, pruritus, or stigmata of chronic liver disease.
clue_3: Bilirubin is predominantly indirect and AST/ALT are not markedly elevated.
clue_4: LDH is high, haptoglobin is low, and the reticulocyte count is increased.
clue_5: Urine lacks conjugated bilirubin despite increased urobilinogen.
clue_6: Peripheral smear or Coombs testing helps identify the hemolytic mechanism.
explanation: Hemolysis overwhelms hepatic conjugation capacity, causing unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, and reticulocytosis.
high_yield: Indirect bilirubin; high LDH; low haptoglobin; high reticulocytes; no bilirubinuria; smear and Coombs help identify cause.

accepted_answers: Gilbert; Gilbert disease; benign unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia
clue_1: A healthy 21-year-old notices mild yellowing of the eyes during final exams after skipping meals.
clue_2: He feels well otherwise, with no abdominal pain, pruritus, dark urine, pale stools, or medication trigger.
clue_3: CBC, reticulocyte count, LDH, haptoglobin, AST, ALT, and ALP are normal.
clue_4: Only unconjugated bilirubin is mildly elevated, usually below 3 mg/dL.
clue_5: The abnormality resolves when stress and fasting end.
clue_6: A benign reduction in UGT1A1 activity explains the intermittent isolated finding.
explanation: Gilbert syndrome is benign reduced bilirubin conjugation causing intermittent isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia triggered by fasting, illness, stress, or exertion.
high_yield: Mild isolated indirect bilirubin; normal CBC and LFTs; fasting/stress/illness trigger; benign UGT1A1 deficiency.

accepted_answers: alcohol-associated hepatitis; alcoholic steatohepatitis
clue_1: A 46-year-old with years of heavy alcohol intake presents with jaundice, anorexia, fever, and tender hepatomegaly.
clue_2: He looks chronically malnourished and has spider angiomas, but the acute complaint is worsening yellowing over weeks.
clue_3: AST is higher than ALT in a ratio above 2, yet AST is under the very high levels typical of acute viral hepatitis.
clue_4: GGT, INR, and bilirubin are elevated; platelets may be low.
clue_5: Ultrasound shows fatty hepatomegaly and no obstructing ductal dilation.
clue_6: Alcohol-related hepatocyte injury with neutrophilic inflammation explains the pattern.
explanation: Alcoholic hepatitis causes acute hepatocellular injury from alcohol with jaundice, tender hepatomegaly, AST:ALT >2:1, elevated GGT, and AST usually under 400.
high_yield: Heavy alcohol use; jaundice; tender hepatomegaly; AST:ALT >2:1; AST rarely >400; elevated GGT/INR; Maddrey DF for severity.

accepted_answers: common bile duct stone; biliary obstruction from gallstone; obstructive choledocholithiasis
clue_1: A 45-year-old with prior biliary colic now has episodic RUQ pain plus dark urine and pale stools.
clue_2: She is uncomfortable but not frankly septic, and fever is absent or minimal.
clue_3: Labs show conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with ALP and GGT out of proportion to transaminases.
clue_4: Ultrasound shows dilation of the common bile duct, while gallbladder wall inflammation is not the main finding.
clue_5: MRCP or EUS would locate the ductal stone; ERCP could remove it.
clue_6: A gallstone lodged in the common bile duct is obstructing bile flow.
explanation: Choledocholithiasis is a gallstone obstructing the common bile duct, producing conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, cholestatic enzymes, dark urine, pale stools, and ductal dilation.
high_yield: RUQ pain + obstructive labs; direct bilirubin and ALP/GGT high; dilated CBD; MRCP/EUS visualizes; ERCP treats.

accepted_answers: pancreatic head adenocarcinoma; pancreatic head malignancy; periampullary pancreatic cancer
clue_1: A 68-year-old presents to clinic for itching, weight loss, and yellowing of the eyes without much abdominal pain.
clue_2: He reports darker urine, pale stools, early satiety, and a new diagnosis of diabetes.
clue_3: Exam finds cachexia and a distended nontender gallbladder.
clue_4: Labs show direct hyperbilirubinemia and elevated ALP, with only modest transaminase changes.
clue_5: CT shows dilation of both the pancreatic duct and common bile duct.
clue_6: A mass at the pancreatic head or periampullary region is compressing the distal biliary tree.
explanation: A pancreatic head mass compresses the distal bile duct, causing painless progressive obstructive jaundice, weight loss, Courvoisier sign, and the double-duct sign.
high_yield: Painless progressive jaundice; weight loss; Courvoisier sign; double duct sign; smoking/chronic pancreatitis/diabetes risk; CA 19-9 not diagnostic alone.

accepted_answers: ascites due to cirrhosis; portal hypertensive ascites; decompensated cirrhosis
clue_1: A 57-year-old with chronic liver disease reports increasing abdominal girth and ankle swelling over months.
clue_2: He has fatigue and muscle wasting despite weight gain from fluid.
clue_3: Exam shows shifting dullness, spider angiomas, palmar erythema, splenomegaly, and possible gynecomastia.
clue_4: Albumin is low, INR is elevated, and platelets are decreased.
clue_5: Diagnostic paracentesis shows SAAG at least 1.1 with low total protein and PMN count below 250.
clue_6: Portal hypertension plus hypoalbuminemia and renal sodium retention is driving the ascites.
explanation: Cirrhosis causes ascites through portal hypertension, hypoalbuminemia, and renal sodium retention, producing high-SAAG, low-protein ascitic fluid.
high_yield: SAAG >=1.1 indicates portal hypertension; cirrhotic ascites has low protein; always tap new/worsening ascites to exclude SBP.

accepted_answers: SBP; infected cirrhotic ascites; spontaneous peritonitis
clue_1: A cirrhotic patient with known ascites becomes more sleepy and says his abdomen feels vaguely sore.
clue_2: He has fever and mild diffuse tenderness, but the exam is less dramatic than a perforated viscus.
clue_3: There is no clear surgical abdomen or polymicrobial source on initial evaluation.
clue_4: Paracentesis shows ascitic PMN count above 250 cells/mm3.
clue_5: Culture may grow a single gut organism such as E. coli or Klebsiella.
clue_6: Low-protein ascitic fluid permits bacterial translocation and infection without an obvious perforation.
explanation: SBP is monomicrobial infection of cirrhotic ascites due to bacterial translocation, diagnosed by ascitic PMNs over 250 cells/mm3.
high_yield: Cirrhotic ascites + fever/pain/encephalopathy; PMN >250 = treat; low-protein ascites risk; distinguish from secondary peritonitis.

accepted_answers: diverticular hemorrhage; bleeding colonic diverticulosis
clue_1: A 76-year-old suddenly passes a large amount of maroon blood while watching television, with no preceding abdominal pain.
clue_2: He takes aspirin and has chronic constipation, but he denies fever, tenesmus, or weeks of bowel habit change.
clue_3: The abdomen is soft and nontender even though he becomes lightheaded from blood loss.
clue_4: Hemoglobin falls quickly; BUN is not the dominant clue unless bleeding is massive or upper source is considered.
clue_5: Colonoscopy after preparation may identify a bleeding vessel inside a diverticulum.
clue_6: Rupture of a vasa recta associated with diverticula causes painless arterial lower GI bleeding.
explanation: Diverticular bleeding is arterial rupture of vasa recta at diverticula, causing sudden painless large-volume hematochezia in older adults.
high_yield: Most common significant adult LGIB; painless massive hematochezia; soft nontender abdomen; colonoscopy/CTA/tagged RBC depending stability.

accepted_answers: colon cancer; rectal cancer; colorectal adenocarcinoma; CRC
clue_1: A 62-year-old reports months of fatigue and subtle change in bowel routine, alternating constipation with looser stools.
clue_2: He has unintentionally lost weight and occasionally sees blood mixed with stool rather than just on paper.
clue_3: Labs show iron deficiency anemia; family history includes colon cancer in a first-degree relative.
clue_4: Rectal exam or colonoscopy may identify a mass, while CEA is for monitoring rather than screening.
clue_5: CT or contrast imaging can show an annular narrowing if left-sided.
clue_6: A mucosal adenocarcinoma explains chronic occult bleeding and progressive luminal narrowing.
explanation: Colorectal cancer causes occult or visible bleeding, iron deficiency anemia, weight loss, and progressive changes in bowel habits or obstruction.
high_yield: Age >50; weight loss; change in bowel habits; iron deficiency anemia; blood mixed with stool; apple-core lesion; colonoscopy with biopsy.

accepted_answers: colonic ischemia; ischemic colopathy
clue_1: A 79-year-old with vascular disease develops sudden crampy left-sided abdominal pain after a hypotensive episode.
clue_2: Several hours later she passes bloody stool, so the pain clearly comes before the bleeding.
clue_3: Exam shows left lower quadrant tenderness but not the soft painless abdomen of diverticular bleeding.
clue_4: Labs show leukocytosis; lactate rises if injury is severe or transmural.
clue_5: CT shows segmental colonic wall thickening in a watershed area with thumbprinting.
clue_6: Transient low flow to the colon causes mucosal ischemia and hematochezia.
explanation: Ischemic colitis is hypoperfusion of vulnerable watershed colon, causing crampy left-sided pain followed by hematochezia and segmental thumbprinting.
high_yield: Pain before bleeding; elderly vascular patient; watershed areas; thumbprinting; segmental mucosal injury; lactate/peritonitis suggests severe ischemia.

accepted_answers: UC; ulcerative proctocolitis; mucosal inflammatory bowel disease
clue_1: A 26-year-old has recurrent episodes of bloody diarrhea with urgency and a feeling of incomplete evacuation.
clue_2: Symptoms improve and relapse over months, with lower abdominal cramping rather than isolated perianal disease.
clue_3: Exam may show fever during flares and extraintestinal findings such as episcleritis or erythema nodosum.
clue_4: Inflammatory markers and fecal calprotectin are elevated; pANCA may be positive.
clue_5: Colonoscopy shows continuous friable inflammation beginning at the rectum with crypt abscesses.
clue_6: The inflammation is mucosal, colonic, and continuous, explaining bleeding and tenesmus.
explanation: Ulcerative colitis is continuous mucosal inflammation beginning in the rectum, causing bloody diarrhea, urgency, tenesmus, crypt abscesses, and PSC association.
high_yield: Bloody diarrhea; rectum always involved; continuous disease; mucosal/submucosal only; pANCA; PSC; no fistulas/skip lesions.

accepted_answers: hemorrhoids; bleeding internal hemorrhoids; painless hemorrhoidal bleeding
clue_1: A 36-year-old notices bright red blood on toilet paper and a few drops in the bowl after straining.
clue_2: He denies weight loss, fever, diarrhea, nocturnal symptoms, or blood mixed throughout the stool.
clue_3: Pain is minimal, though he feels intermittent prolapse after bowel movements.
clue_4: External inspection is unrevealing, but anoscopy shows engorged cushions above the dentate line.
clue_5: CBC is normal and abdominal exam is benign.
clue_6: Dilated internal hemorrhoidal venous cushions bleed painlessly because they are above the pain-sensitive anoderm.
explanation: Internal hemorrhoids are dilated submucosal vascular cushions above the dentate line, causing painless bright red blood on paper or coating stool.
high_yield: Blood on toilet paper/coating stool; painless if internal; constipation/straining/pregnancy; anoscopy; do not assume in older patients without workup.

accepted_answers: posterior midline fissure; painful anal fissure
clue_1: A 31-year-old avoids bowel movements because each one causes sharp tearing pain that lasts afterward.
clue_2: He sees a small amount of bright red blood on the paper after passing hard stool.
clue_3: The bleeding is not mixed with stool and there is no systemic illness.
clue_4: Gentle inspection reveals a linear posterior midline tear with a small sentinel tag.
clue_5: Pain limits digital rectal exam; anoscopy may be deferred until symptoms improve.
clue_6: A split in the anoderm below the dentate line explains severe defecation pain.
explanation: An anal fissure is a tear in the anoderm, usually posterior midline, producing severe pain with defecation and small bright red bleeding.
high_yield: Severe defecation pain; posterior midline tear; small BRBPR on paper; constipation; off-midline suggests Crohn/TB/syphilis.

accepted_answers: SBO; adhesive small bowel obstruction; mechanical small bowel obstruction
clue_1: A 64-year-old with prior abdominal surgery develops waves of crampy central abdominal pain and repeated vomiting.
clue_2: He has stopped passing flatus, and the abdomen becomes distended and tympanitic.
clue_3: Vomitus is bilious rather than feculent early in the course.
clue_4: Exam may reveal high-pitched bowel sounds initially, later quieter if severe.
clue_5: CT shows dilated small-bowel loops with a transition point and little colonic gas.
clue_6: Adhesions from earlier surgery are the most likely mechanical cause.
explanation: SBO is mechanical blockage of small bowel, most often adhesions after surgery, producing colicky pain, bilious vomiting, distension, obstipation, and air-fluid levels.
high_yield: Prior surgery/adhesions; colicky pain; bilious vomiting; obstipation; dilated small bowel + transition point; lactate/peritonitis = strangulation.

accepted_answers: LBO; colonic obstruction; obstructing colon cancer
clue_1: A 72-year-old develops progressive abdominal distension and inability to pass stool or gas, with vomiting only late.
clue_2: The pain is lower abdominal and crampy rather than early bilious vomiting.
clue_3: Exam shows a massively tympanitic abdomen and possible rectal mass or occult blood.
clue_4: Abdominal imaging shows colonic dilation proximal to a distal blockage with collapsed distal colon.
clue_5: Cecal diameter becomes important because perforation risk rises as it approaches or exceeds 12 cm.
clue_6: The obstruction is in the colon, commonly from cancer, volvulus, or diverticular stricture.
explanation: LBO causes distal colonic blockage with massive distension, obstipation, late vomiting, and a transition point, commonly from colorectal cancer.
high_yield: Massive distension; obstipation; late feculent vomiting; CRC/volvulus/diverticular stricture; cecum >12 cm risks perforation.

accepted_answers: adult sigmoid volvulus; sigmoid colon volvulus
clue_1: An elderly nursing-home resident with chronic constipation develops dramatic abdominal distension and obstipation.
clue_2: Pain is crampy and lower abdominal, and vomiting is a late feature.
clue_3: He has a tympanitic abdomen but may initially lack peritoneal signs.
clue_4: Plain film shows a massively dilated loop arising from the pelvis with a bent inner wall pattern.
clue_5: CT or contrast study confirms torsion of the sigmoid colon.
clue_6: Endoscopic detorsion is attempted if there is no perforation or ischemia.
explanation: Sigmoid volvulus is axial twisting of the sigmoid colon causing closed-loop large bowel obstruction, classically in elderly institutionalized constipated patients.
high_yield: Elderly/institutionalized; chronic constipation; massive distension + obstipation; coffee bean to RUQ; endoscopic detorsion unless ischemia/perforation.

accepted_answers: cecal volvulus; mobile cecum volvulus
clue_1: A 38-year-old with prior abdominal surgery develops sudden colicky pain, distension, and nausea.
clue_2: Unlike sigmoid volvulus, he is younger and the abnormal loop is not arising from the pelvis.
clue_3: Exam shows tympany with right-sided or diffuse tenderness.
clue_4: Imaging shows a dilated cecum displaced toward the left upper abdomen, sometimes kidney-bean shaped.
clue_5: There is risk of closed-loop obstruction and ischemia, so endoscopic decompression is less reliable.
clue_6: A mobile cecum has twisted on its mesentery.
explanation: Cecal volvulus is twisting of a mobile cecum, causing closed-loop obstruction in a younger patient, often with kidney-bean/comma sign displaced to the LUQ.
high_yield: Younger patient; mobile cecum/prior surgery/pregnancy; kidney bean in LUQ; requires surgery rather than endoscopic detorsion.

accepted_answers: celiac sprue; gluten-sensitive enteropathy; gluten enteropathy
clue_1: A 29-year-old reports years of bloating, fatigue, and bulky foul-smelling stools that float, but no visible blood.
clue_2: She has iron deficiency despite adequate intake and intermittent aphthous ulcers.
clue_3: Exam may show pallor, cheilosis, or a pruritic vesicular rash on extensor surfaces.
clue_4: Anti-tTG IgA is positive, with total IgA checked to avoid a false negative.
clue_5: Duodenal biopsy shows villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes.
clue_6: Gluten-triggered immune injury to small bowel mucosa causes malabsorption.
explanation: Celiac disease is immune reaction to gluten causing small-bowel villous atrophy, malabsorption, steatorrhea, iron/folate deficiency, and dermatitis herpetiformis.
high_yield: Steatorrhea; iron/folate deficiency; dermatitis herpetiformis; anti-tTG IgA + total IgA; duodenal biopsy confirms; gluten-free diet.

accepted_answers: IBS; irritable bowel syndrome diarrhea predominant; functional bowel disorder
clue_1: A 33-year-old has recurrent crampy abdominal pain with alternating constipation and loose stools for years.
clue_2: Pain often improves after defecation and worsens during stressful weeks.
clue_3: She has no weight loss, fever, GI bleeding, anemia, nocturnal diarrhea, or abnormal exam.
clue_4: CBC, CRP, TSH, celiac serology, and stool studies are unrevealing.
clue_5: Symptoms meet a chronic pattern linked to stool frequency/form rather than structural disease.
clue_6: A disorder of gut-brain interaction and visceral hypersensitivity explains the presentation.
explanation: IBS is a disorder of gut-brain interaction with chronic recurrent abdominal pain related to defecation and changes in stool frequency/form, without inflammatory or structural disease.
high_yield: Pain relieved/related to defecation; no nocturnal symptoms; normal labs; no blood/weight loss/anemia; Rome IV; diagnosis after excluding red flags.

accepted_answers: C difficile; C diff colitis; pseudomembranous colitis
clue_1: A 69-year-old develops frequent watery diarrhea a week after discharge for pneumonia treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics.
clue_2: He has lower abdominal cramping and fever but no travel or undercooked food exposure.
clue_3: White count is markedly elevated and creatinine is above baseline.
clue_4: Stool testing detects toxigenic C. difficile; colonoscopy is usually unnecessary.

clue_3: EGD reveals broad serpiginous ulcers rather than many small volcano-like ulcers.
clue_4: Histology demonstrates viral cytopathic effect with intranuclear and intracytoplasmic inclusions.
clue_5: The biopsy site matters because infected stromal/endothelial cells are often at the ulcer base.
clue_6: Reactivation of CMV produces large distal esophageal ulcers.
explanation: CMV esophagitis causes odynophagia in severely immunocompromised patients with large shallow linear distal ulcers and owl-eye inclusions.
high_yield: Immunocompromised odynophagia; no thrush; large linear distal ulcers; owl-eye inclusions; treat ganciclovir.

accepted_answers: herpes simplex esophagitis; HSV infectious esophagitis
clue_1: A patient receiving chemotherapy has painful swallowing and clusters of oral vesicles.
clue_2: Symptoms are more painful than obstructive, and food sticking is secondary.
clue_3: Endoscopy reveals discrete volcano-like ulcers rather than long linear distal lesions.
clue_4: Tzanck-like cytology would show multinucleation and nuclear molding.
clue_5: CMV would be favored by large shallow linear ulcers with owl-eye cells.
clue_6: HSV infects squamous epithelium and causes punched-out esophageal ulcers.
explanation: HSV esophagitis is opportunistic viral esophageal infection causing odynophagia with multiple small punched-out mid-esophageal ulcers and multinucleated giant cells.
high_yield: Odynophagia; immunocompromised; punched-out/volcano ulcers; multinucleated giant cells and Cowdry A; treat acyclovir.
